title: What MrBeast Misses About The 'Scary Times' For Creators And AI
text:
Innovation
Social Media
What MrBeast Misses About The 'Scary Times' For Creators And AI
By
John Brandon
,
Senior Contributor.
Forbes contributors publish independent expert analyses and insights.
John Brandon covers social media trends. @johnbrandonmn
Follow Author
Oct 10, 2025, 10:55am EDT
Oct 10, 2025, 03:38pm EDT
ELMONT, NEW YORK - SEPTEMBER 07: MrBeast attends the 2025 MTV Video Music Awards at UBS Arena on September 07, 2025 in Elmont, New York. (Photo by Roy Rochlin/Getty Images for MTV)
Getty Images for MTV
When MrBeast speaks, people listen. And, honestly, he speaks quite often about topics that are trending and worth discussing. Recently, the prolific and top-rated creator who has 444 million followers on YouTube
posted on X
saying we’re in “scary times” and that
AI content is coming for all of the millions and millions of creators out there
.
(Interestingly, he chose X as the platform to share this opinion. The original public square we once called Twitter
does foster some excellent debate at times
.)
The comments have been all over the board, with a few people criticizing MrBeast because
he has dabbled in AI himself
. Yet, most of the replies have been either lamenting the inevitable nature of AI—that it will soon become indistinguishable from real content—or suggesting it has a long way to go.
My favorite comment so far
was about MrBeast’s own content
and how it would be hard for an AI to replicate what he does. It serves as an excellent example of why AI might not take over all social media channels quite so soon.
How MrBeast Mobilizes Real People
What that commenter on X means is that we all know when a video involves real people doing something incredible, and we’re drawn to that realism.
MORE FOR YOU
This is why theatrical movies that rely too heavily on CGI become nothing but digital noise. We know it’s not real and we don’t care what happens. When Tom Cruise actually flies in an airplane and almost falls out, we stay glued to the screen. AI content is currently quite realistic and convincing, but not that different from CGI.
Recent studies about how much AI content is on social media seem overstated to me. One claimed that by next year,
48% of all content will be AI generated
. The survey profiled marketing experts, yet it’s impossible to know how much content is made by an AI. For starters, our detection prowess is still at an early stage. What’s actually happening is a little less scary:
people are using AI almost like an unpaid intern
. It’s often used to augment what real creators are doing, not completely replace them.
That’s why I’m not worried about the “scary times” comment quite yet. There is no human element to any of it right now; AI content generation is still relatively easy to spot and exists only on a computer. Even if there was a YouTube video showing hundreds of people gathered to compete in a zany challenge, we’d know it’s fake.
Meanwhile, MrBeast can mobilize his followers like no one else in recent memory. That has a snowball effect for content created by and with real people. Even if only a few dozen people post about the experience, you can still reach thousands quite quickly.
That’s the real power of social media. When you share a video with only 1,000 followers but everyone reshares the post, you suddenly can reach a massive audience. The secret is often whether your post is appealing and compelling enough to reshare.
When the influencer is someone like MrBeast, it’s even more pronounced.
A MrBeast video about being in solitary confinement or a real life squid game goes viral when everyone starts sharing it—starting with the people who were
actually in the video
. An AI can certainly generate that kind of buzz, but without real people involved, it’s extremely hard for AI to replicate—if not impossible.
Why AI Has A Long Way To Go
AI bots might have millions of users and
there’s even an AI actress who is popular on Instagram
, but there is no relational component. There are no real people who are mobilized in the videos, excited about what just happened in real life. It’s all fake.
It will take many years or even decades before AI can interact in the real world, likely with a humanoid bot of some kind
like this one from Tesla
. Even then, the creators who work so hard on real content, who physically go to a location and film with real people, who labor over Photoshop stills for hours—I don’t think they’re easy to replace.
Meanwhile, that AI actress I mentioned serves as a good example of why we’re not in scary times yet. Sure, the bot might eventually end up in a movie someday, but I can imagine how the press conference promoting the film might go. It will be more like a boring Zoom call. There’s no soul to the content, no physicality, and no emotion.
Another disadvantage to AI is that real content has a long tail. Humans have been creating video content for many, many decades. It has served as the foundation of AI content to be sure but also has a long and fruitful history of involving real people, not just imaginary content. AI does not have and will never have a fruitful history involving real people.
A good example of this? The actress who was in the original Jaws movie in the opening scene
once talked about what it was like to be on set and work with Steven Spielberg
. No one will ever talk about how wonderful it is to work with an AI bot. There are no real emotions and no family connections—just a soulless entity spewing out digital frames.
For social media users, the lack of human connection with AI means it could be many years before MrBeast is right about “scary times” with content creation—and even then, we all know when something doesn’t have a soul, right?
